meta.title: MLOps
meta.subtitle: productionise machine learning
meta.blurb: The practices and platforms that take ML models from notebook experiments to reliable, monitored, continuously-improving production systems.

t-mlops-intro.label: What is MLOps?
t-mlops-intro.desc: The discipline of deploying and maintaining ML models in production reliably and efficiently.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **MLOps** applies DevOps principles to machine learning: automate the path from experiment to production, monitor models in the wild, and retrain when performance degrades. It bridges the gap between 'model works in a notebook' and 'model works in production'.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: MLOps spans **data management**, **experiment tracking**, **model packaging**, **deployment**, **monitoring**, and **retraining**. Unlike traditional software, ML systems degrade silently as the world changes — so continuous monitoring and feedback loops are essential, not optional.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Google defines three maturity levels: **Level 0** (manual), **Level 1** (ML pipeline automation), **Level 2** (CI/CD for pipelines). Most teams are between 0 and 1. The goal isn't maximum automation — it's reliable, reproducible, auditable model delivery at your team's scale.

t-ml-lifecycle.label: ML Project Lifecycle
t-ml-lifecycle.desc: The stages of an ML project — from problem framing through deployment to continuous improvement.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: The lifecycle: **scope** (define the problem) → **data** (collect, label, validate) → **model** (train, tune, evaluate) → **deploy** (serve, monitor, iterate). Most time is spent on data, not models.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Each stage feeds back: monitoring reveals data drift → triggers retraining → new data collection. **Error analysis** after deployment guides what to improve next. The lifecycle is a loop, not a line.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Google's Rule #1: 'Don't be afraid to launch a product without ML.' Start with baselines and heuristics. Add ML only when you have enough data and a clear metric. Then iterate: the first model is never the last.

t-reproducibility.label: Reproducibility
t-reproducibility.desc: Ensuring experiments and training runs can be exactly repeated — code, data, environment, and config.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A result is **reproducible** if you can re-run the same code on the same data and get the same output. This requires pinning: code version, data version, random seeds, dependencies, and hardware.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Version **everything**: code (git), data (DVC, lakeFS), configs (YAML tracked in git), environment (Docker, conda lock). Log the full context with every experiment run. Without this, debugging production issues becomes guesswork.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Perfect reproducibility is sometimes impossible (GPU non-determinism, external API changes). Aim for **practical reproducibility**: same code + same data + same config = results within acceptable variance. Document known sources of non-determinism.

t-versioning.label: Data & Model Versioning
t-versioning.desc: Tracking changes to datasets and models over time — the ML equivalent of git for code.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Code has git. Data and models need versioning too: know which dataset trained which model, and roll back when something breaks.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **DVC** tracks large files alongside git (pointers in git, data in remote storage). **lakeFS** provides git-like branching for data lakes. **Model registries** (MLflow, Vertex AI) version model artifacts with metadata, lineage, and stage transitions (staging → production).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: At minimum: tag every training run with its data snapshot hash and code commit. For regulated industries, full data lineage and model provenance are compliance requirements, not nice-to-haves.

t-experiment-tracking.label: Experiment Tracking
t-experiment-tracking.desc: Logging parameters, metrics, artifacts, and code for every training run — so you can compare and reproduce.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Log every experiment: **parameters** (learning rate, batch size), **metrics** (loss, accuracy over time), **artifacts** (model files, plots), and **code version**. Without this, you'll forget what worked and why.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Tools: **MLflow 3** (open-source, broad ecosystem, now with GenAI tracing for LLM workloads), **W&B** (rich visualisation, team collaboration, LLM-specific prompt tracking), **Neptune** (flexible metadata). All provide dashboards for comparing runs, searching by metric, and linking experiments to deployed models.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Instrument tracking from day one — it's trivial to add early and painful to retrofit. Log not just final metrics but **training curves**, **data statistics**, and **hardware utilisation**. Use experiment tags and notes liberally; future-you will thank present-you.

t-hyperparameter.label: Hyperparameter Tuning
t-hyperparameter.desc: Systematically searching for the best model configuration — grid, random, Bayesian, and multi-fidelity methods.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Hyperparameters (learning rate, layers, regularisation) aren't learned from data — you choose them. **Tuning** systematically searches for the combination that gives the best validation performance.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Grid search** (exhaustive but expensive), **random search** (surprisingly effective), **Bayesian optimisation** (smart, iterative — Optuna, Hyperopt). **Multi-fidelity** methods (Hyperband, ASHA) train cheaply first, promoting only promising configs.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Start with random search to understand the landscape, then Bayesian for fine-tuning. Use **early stopping** aggressively — most of the search budget is wasted on bad configs. For large models, tune learning rate and batch size first; they dominate performance. Track all trials in your experiment tracker.

t-distributed-training.label: Distributed Training
t-distributed-training.desc: Training models across multiple GPUs or machines — data parallelism, model parallelism, and mixed strategies.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: When a model or dataset doesn't fit on one GPU, distribute training across multiple. **Data parallelism**: same model on each GPU, different data batches, gradients synced. Most common and simplest to set up.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Model parallelism** splits the model itself across GPUs (needed for very large models). **FSDP** (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) and **DeepSpeed ZeRO** shard both model state and gradients for memory efficiency. **Pipeline parallelism** splits layers across devices.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: For most use cases, **DDP** (Distributed Data Parallel) with PyTorch or **Hugging Face Accelerate** handles multi-GPU training with minimal code changes. For LLM-scale training, you need DeepSpeed/FSDP + tensor parallelism. The hard part is debugging distributed failures and optimising communication overhead.

t-feature-eng.label: Feature Engineering & Stores
t-feature-eng.desc: Creating, managing, and serving ML features consistently across training and inference.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Features** are the inputs to your model. **Feature engineering** transforms raw data into informative signals. A **feature store** ensures the same features used in training are served identically at inference time.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Feature pipelines compute features (batch or streaming) and write to both an **offline store** (for training) and **online store** (for serving). This prevents **training-serving skew** — the #1 silent failure in production ML.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: You don't always need a full feature store. For simple models with batch features, a well-governed dbt layer suffices. Feature stores shine when: features are shared across many models, real-time serving is needed, or you must guarantee consistency. Feast (open-source) or Tecton (managed) are the main options.

t-model-packaging.label: Model Packaging
t-model-packaging.desc: Bundling a trained model with its dependencies into a deployable, portable artifact.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A deployable model needs: the **model artifact** (weights), **inference code** (pre/post-processing), and **dependencies** (libraries, versions). Package these together so deployment is a single unit, not a collection of steps.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Formats: **ONNX** (portable across frameworks), **TorchScript** (PyTorch native), **SavedModel** (TensorFlow). Containerise with Docker for environment isolation. MLflow's model flavours provide a standard packaging interface across frameworks.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Optimise before deploying: **quantisation** (INT8/FP16, 2–4x speedup), **pruning** (remove redundant weights), **distillation** (smaller student model). Use **ONNX Runtime** or **TensorRT** for inference optimisation. The packaging step is where you trade training flexibility for serving efficiency.

t-model-registry.label: Model Registry
t-model-registry.desc: A central catalog of trained models — versioned, staged, and approved for deployment.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **model registry** is a versioned catalog of production-ready models. It tracks which model version is in staging, production, or archived, along with metadata (who trained it, on what data, with what metrics).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Workflow: experiment → register best model → **staging** (validate) → **production** (serve) → **archived** (replaced). Attach lineage: training data hash, code commit, evaluation metrics, approval records.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The registry is the handoff point between training and serving teams. Enforce approval gates before promotion to production (automated tests + human sign-off for high-stakes models). MLflow Registry works for most teams; cloud-native options (Vertex, SageMaker) integrate tighter with their serving infrastructure.

t-cicd-ml.label: CI/CD for ML
t-cicd-ml.desc: Continuous integration and delivery adapted for ML — testing code, data, and models in automated pipelines.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **CI for ML** validates code changes (unit tests, linting) AND data/model quality (schema checks, performance regression tests). **CD for ML** automates deployment of validated models to serving infrastructure.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: ML CI adds: **data validation** (schema, statistics drift), **training pipeline tests** (runs a fast training on sample data), **model quality gates** (metric thresholds). CD adds: **shadow deployment** (run new model alongside old, compare), **canary rollout** (gradual traffic shift).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Don't try to replicate full training in CI — it's too slow and expensive. Test with small data samples and fast-training configs. Gate promotion on offline eval metrics. Use shadow mode or A/B tests for online validation before full rollout. Automate rollback triggers on serving metric degradation.

t-testing-ml.label: Testing ML Systems
t-testing-ml.desc: What to test in ML beyond code — data tests, model tests, and integration tests for the full pipeline.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: ML systems need three kinds of tests: **code tests** (logic correctness), **data tests** (schema, distributions, freshness), and **model tests** (performance, fairness, robustness). Most teams only do the first, if any.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Data tests**: null rates, range checks, feature distributions match expectations. **Model tests**: metrics above threshold, no regression vs baseline, performance across slices (demographic groups, edge cases). **Integration tests**: end-to-end pipeline produces valid predictions.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Google's ML Test Score paper identifies 28 specific tests for production ML systems. Start with the highest-value ones: input data validation, prediction sanity checks (not all same class, within expected range), and performance regression on a held-out eval set. Add slice-based analysis for fairness-critical applications.

t-serving-patterns.label: Model Serving Patterns
t-serving-patterns.desc: How to expose models for inference — online, batch, streaming, and embedded patterns.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Three main patterns: **Online** (real-time request-response, <100ms), **Batch** (score a large dataset periodically), **Streaming** (score events as they flow through Kafka/Flink). Choose based on latency requirements and traffic patterns.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Online serving: REST/gRPC endpoint behind a load balancer. Batch: scheduled Spark/Python job writing predictions to a table. Streaming: model embedded in a Flink/Kafka Streams job. **Edge/embedded**: model runs on-device (mobile, IoT) for offline capability and low latency.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Most ML in production is actually **batch** (score overnight, serve pre-computed results via lookup). Real-time serving is expensive and complex — use it only when freshness truly matters (fraud, recommendations, search ranking). Pre-compute what you can, serve real-time only what you must.

t-batch-inference.label: Batch Inference
t-batch-inference.desc: Scoring large datasets offline on a schedule — the simplest and most common serving pattern.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Run the model over a large dataset on a schedule (hourly/daily), write predictions to a database or file. Consumers read pre-computed results. Simple, cost-effective, and no latency pressure.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Run with Spark for distributed inference on huge datasets, or simple Python scripts for moderate sizes. Store results in a warehouse table or key-value store for fast lookup. Schedule with your orchestrator (Airflow, Dagster).

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Batch inference powers most recommendation systems, risk scores, and propensity models. The trade-off is **freshness** — predictions are stale until the next run. Mitigate with more frequent runs or a hybrid approach (batch for most, real-time for time-sensitive signals).

t-realtime-inference.label: Real-Time Inference
t-realtime-inference.desc: Serving predictions on demand with low latency — infrastructure, optimization, and scaling challenges.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A model behind an API endpoint: receive a request, run inference, return the prediction in milliseconds. Requires always-on infrastructure, careful latency management, and auto-scaling.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Inference servers: **Triton** (multi-framework, GPU batching), **vLLM** (LLM-optimised, paged attention), **TorchServe**, **TF Serving**. Optimise with: model quantisation, dynamic batching (accumulate requests for GPU efficiency), response caching for repeated inputs.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Real-time ML at scale is expensive. Strategies: **right-size instances** (GPU for large models, CPU for small), **auto-scale on queue depth** (not just CPU), **multi-model serving** (share infrastructure). For LLMs, **vLLM** with continuous batching, PagedAttention, and speculative decoding dramatically improves throughput/cost. **SGLang** is emerging as an alternative for structured generation. Always set latency SLOs and alert on p99, not just average.

t-gpu-infra.label: GPU Infrastructure
t-gpu-infra.desc: Managing GPU compute for training and inference — instance types, scheduling, cost, and utilisation.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: GPUs accelerate ML workloads 10–100x over CPUs due to massive parallelism. Training large models requires high-end GPUs (A100, H100); inference can often use smaller/older GPUs or even CPUs for simpler models.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Instance types**: A10G (inference sweet spot), L4 (cost-effective inference), A100/H100 (training workhorses), **H200** (1.5x H100 memory bandwidth), **B100/B200** (next-gen Blackwell, 2.5x H100 training throughput). Use **spot/preemptible** instances for training (60–90% savings, handle interruptions with checkpointing). **Multi-Instance GPU (MIG)** slices one GPU for multiple workloads.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: GPU utilisation is typically 30–50% in most orgs — massive waste. Improve with: job scheduling (Kubernetes + GPU quotas), right-sizing (don't use H100 for inference of a small model), and time-sharing. In 2026, GPU demand outpaces supply — cost optimisation is a core MLOps skill, not a nice-to-have.

t-model-monitoring.label: Model Monitoring
t-model-monitoring.desc: Watching model performance in production — detecting degradation before users notice.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Models degrade silently in production as the world changes. **Monitor**: prediction distributions, input feature distributions, and (when available) actual outcomes vs predictions. Alert when metrics breach thresholds.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Performance monitoring** (accuracy, AUC — needs ground truth, often delayed). **Proxy monitoring** (prediction drift, confidence distributions — no ground truth needed, faster signal). Tools: **Evidently**, **NannyML**, **WhyLabs**, cloud-native monitors.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Ground truth is often delayed (days/weeks for some tasks). Use **proxy metrics** (drift, confidence drops) for early warning, and true performance metrics once labels arrive. Set up automatic retraining triggers, but always validate before auto-promoting. Monitor per-segment, not just globally — degradation often hides in subgroups.

t-data-drift.label: Data & Concept Drift
t-data-drift.desc: When the data changes after deployment — detecting and responding to distribution shifts.

links.title: Failing Loudly: An Empirical Study of Methods for Detecting Dataset Shift

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Data drift**: input features change distribution (e.g., new user demographics). **Concept drift**: the relationship between inputs and outputs changes (e.g., user preferences shift). Both silently degrade model performance.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Covariate shift**: P(X) changes but P(Y|X) stays the same. **Concept drift**: P(Y|X) itself changes. Detect with statistical tests (KS test, PSI, Jensen-Shannon divergence) comparing production data to training data distributions.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Not all drift matters — only drift that impacts predictions is actionable. Monitor drift AND performance together. Response strategies: **retrain on recent data** (most common), **online learning** (continuous updates), or **ensemble with recency weighting**. Seasonal patterns aren't drift — distinguish recurring patterns from genuine shifts.

t-ab-testing.label: A/B Testing & Experimentation
t-ab-testing.desc: Comparing model variants in production with controlled experiments to measure real-world impact.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An **A/B test** splits live traffic between model variants (control vs treatment) and measures which performs better on a business metric. The only reliable way to prove a new model actually improves outcomes.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Design: define **primary metric** (what you're optimising), **guardrail metrics** (what must not regress), **sample size** (statistical power), and **duration**. Avoid peeking — wait for statistical significance. **Interleaving** is more sample-efficient for ranking models.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Offline metrics (AUC, RMSE) don't always predict online impact. A/B tests close this gap but are slow. Use **multi-armed bandits** for faster convergence when you can tolerate some exploration cost. Always run guardrails — a model that improves clicks but degrades revenue is a net loss.

t-ml-governance.label: ML Governance & Responsible AI
t-ml-governance.desc: Ensuring ML systems are fair, explainable, auditable, and compliant with regulations.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: ML governance ensures models are **fair** (no harmful bias), **explainable** (stakeholders understand decisions), **auditable** (full lineage), and **compliant** (regulations like the EU AI Act). Not optional for production systems.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Model cards** document intended use, limitations, and performance across demographic groups. **Fairness metrics** (equal opportunity, demographic parity) quantify bias. **Explainability** tools (SHAP, LIME) help interpret predictions. **Audit trails** track every model change and approval.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The EU AI Act (enforced 2025–2026) classifies AI systems by risk level and mandates documentation, testing, and human oversight for high-risk applications. Even outside regulation, governance builds trust. Automate: fairness checks in CI, model cards generated from registry metadata, and mandatory human approval for high-stakes model deployments.

t-ml-platforms.label: ML Platforms
t-ml-platforms.desc: End-to-end platforms that provide infrastructure for the full ML lifecycle — managed and self-built.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An **ML platform** provides integrated tooling for the full lifecycle: notebooks, training infrastructure, experiment tracking, model registry, serving, and monitoring. Build vs buy is the core decision.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Managed platforms**: SageMaker (AWS-native, broadest), Vertex AI (GCP, strong on AutoML), Databricks (unified with data). **Open-source stack**: MLflow + Kubeflow/Metaflow + Seldon/BentoML + Feast + Evidently. Most teams land somewhere between: managed compute + open-source tooling.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Don't build a platform until you've outgrown stitching tools together. Start with MLflow (tracking + registry), a cloud notebook service, and managed serving. Add orchestration and feature stores as complexity demands. Internal ML platforms are expensive to build and maintain — justify them with scale (many teams, many models).

t-ml-pipelines.label: ML Pipelines
t-ml-pipelines.desc: Automated, reproducible workflows that chain data prep, training, evaluation, and deployment into a single DAG.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An **ML pipeline** automates the sequence: fetch data → preprocess → train → evaluate → register → deploy. Each step is a reusable, testable component. Pipelines make training **reproducible and triggerable** (on schedule, on data change, on demand).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Kubeflow Pipelines**: Kubernetes-native, great for complex DAGs. **Metaflow**: Netflix's human-centric framework (Python decorators, versioned, cloud-aware). **SageMaker/Vertex Pipelines**: cloud-integrated. **ZenML**: abstracts across infrastructure.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Don't over-engineer pipelines early. Start with a script that trains and evaluates; wrap it in a simple orchestrator (Airflow task, GitHub Action). Graduate to Kubeflow/Metaflow when you need: caching expensive steps, parallel hyperparameter sweeps, or multiple teams sharing pipeline components. The pipeline should be the **single path to production** — no manual notebook-to-deployment shortcuts.

t-automl.label: AutoML
t-automl.desc: Automated machine learning — automating model selection, feature engineering, and hyperparameter tuning.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **AutoML** automates the tedious parts of ML: trying different algorithms, tuning hyperparameters, engineering features, and selecting the best model. You provide data and a target; it returns a trained model.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Tools: **AutoGluon** (best tabular AutoML, ensemble-based), **FLAML** (fast, lightweight), **Vertex/SageMaker AutoML** (managed, for non-experts). Techniques: automated feature engineering, neural architecture search (NAS), meta-learning.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: AutoML is excellent for: establishing baselines quickly, tabular data tasks, and teams without deep ML expertise. It's less useful for: custom architectures, highly domain-specific problems, or when you need full control over the model. Use it as a **starting point**, not a replacement for understanding your problem.

t-llmops.label: LLMOps
t-llmops.desc: Operating LLM-based applications in production — prompt management, evaluation, cost, and guardrails.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **LLMOps** adapts MLOps for LLM applications: managing **prompts** (versioned, tested), **evaluating** outputs (quality, safety, groundedness), controlling **cost** (token usage, caching), and enforcing **guardrails** (content safety, schema compliance).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Prompt management: version prompts, A/B test variants, track performance. Evaluation: LLM-as-judge, human eval, regression tests on golden sets. Cost: prompt caching, model routing (cheap model for simple queries, expensive for complex), output length control. Observability: trace every chain/agent step.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: LLMOps adapts MLOps for LLM-specific challenges: **prompt sensitivity** (small prompt changes = large output shifts), **token cost management** (orders of magnitude more expensive than classical ML), **hallucination risk** (factual grounding is not guaranteed), and **evaluation difficulty** (no single metric captures quality). Tools: **Langfuse** and **LangSmith** for tracing, **MLflow 3** for unified tracking, **AI gateways** (Portkey, LiteLLM) for governed model access. The discipline is rapidly maturing in 2026.
